schools.summary: Abimelech going to Shechem to scheme with his mother's kindred: 'and Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren, and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying' — Abimelech, Gideon's son by a concubine, went to plot with his mother's kin (the and Abimelech the son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem unto his mother's brethren, Abimelech to Shechem, the and communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father, saying, the plot begun, the figure, the ambitious son scheming with his kindred for the kingdom, the truth that Abimelech sought the throne by the help of his mother's people at Shechem, Judges 8:31, the figure of the ambition that schemes for power, the kindred enlisted, the plot of Abimelech, the conspiracy begun, vv.31); Abimelech going to his mother's kin (the plot, Judges 8:31) — the going (to Shechem); 'communed with them, and with all the family of the house of his mother's father' (Abimelech scheming with the kindred, the plot, the figure, the ambition that schemes for power, the providence, the plot, the appeal to the men of Shechem to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Abimelech's appeal to the men of Shechem, pleading his kinship: 'speak, I pray you, in the ears of all the men of Shechem, Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal, which are threescore and ten persons, reign over you, or that one reign over you? remember also that I am your bone and your flesh' — Abimelech urged the Shechemites to prefer him, their kinsman, to the seventy (the speak, I pray you, in the ears of all the men of Shechem, Whether is better for you, either that all the sons of Jerubbaal, which are threescore and ten persons, reign over you, or that one reign over you?, the false alternative, the remember also that I am your bone and your flesh, the plea of kinship, the figure, the demagogue's appeal, the false choice and the plea of blood, the truth that Abimelech set a false alternative and pleaded kinship to win the throne, Genesis 29:14, the figure of the ambition that flatters and deceives the people to gain power, the appeal of Abimelech, the plea of kinship, vv.1); Abimelech setting a false alternative (the demagoguery) — the choice (one or seventy); 'remember also that I am your bone and your flesh' (the plea of kinship to win them, the appeal, Genesis 29:14, the figure, the ambition that flatters the people, the providence, the appeal, the men of Shechem inclining after Abimelech to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The Shechemites inclining to follow Abimelech as their brother: 'and his mother's brethren spake of him in the ears of all the men of Shechem all these words: and their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother' — the kindred swayed the men, whose hearts turned to Abimelech as their kinsman (the and his mother's brethren spake of him in the ears of all the men of Shechem all these words, the kindred spoke, the and their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother, their hearts inclined, the figure, the people swayed by kinship rather than right, the heart turned by partiality, the truth that the Shechemites chose Abimelech for blood, not for worth, the heart inclined by partiality, Judges 9:2, the figure of the people swayed by self-interest and kinship to a wrong choice, the inclining of the people, the choice for Abimelech, vv.2); the kindred swaying the men (the persuasion, Judges 9:2) — the speaking (the kindred spoke); 'their hearts inclined to follow Abimelech; for they said, He is our brother' (the hearts inclined by kinship, the partiality, the figure, the people swayed by kinship to a wrong choice, the providence, the inclining, the money given to hire vain men to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Abimelech hiring vain men with silver from Baal's temple: 'and they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith, wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him' — Abimelech hired worthless men with money from the idol's temple (the and they gave him threescore and ten pieces of silver out of the house of Baalberith, silver from Baal's house, the wherewith Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him, vain men hired, the figure, the idol's money buying worthless men for the bloody work, the truth that the wages of the idol-temple bought a band of reckless men, the means as base as the end, Judges 8:33, the figure of the ungodly enterprise furnished by ungodly means, the worthless hired for evil, the hire of Abimelech, the band of vain men, vv.3); the silver from Baal's temple (the means, Judges 8:33) — the silver (from Baalberith); 'Abimelech hired vain and light persons, which followed him' (the worthless men hired for the work, the band, the figure, the ungodly enterprise furnished by ungodly means, the providence, the hire, the slaughter of the seventy to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Abimelech slaying his seventy brethren upon one stone, only Jotham escaping: 'and he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself' — the bloodshed of ambition — 'and he went unto his father's house at Ophrah, and slew his brethren the sons of Jerubbaal, being threescore and ten persons, upon one stone: notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left': Abimelech butchered his seventy brothers on a single stone to clear his path to the throne (the witnesses noting the significance — the horror of an ambition that stops at nothing, the murder of seventy brethren, the sons of the deliverer Gideon, slaughtered like victims on one stone to secure a usurped crown, the basest treachery and cruelty in the pursuit of power, the seventy slain by the hired band, the deliberate butchery on one stone, the deepest ingratitude against Gideon's house and the basest crime against kindred, the lesson — that ambition unrestrained by the fear of God becomes murderous, trampling every bond of nature and blood to grasp at power, 'they that will be rich fall into... many foolish and hurtful lusts,' 1 Timothy 6:9-10, the truth that the love of power, like the love of money, is a root of all evil, leading men to the foulest crimes, 'from whence come wars and fightings... ye lust, and have not: ye kill,' James 4:1-2, the figure — the cruelty of the wicked who shed innocent blood to advance themselves, the type of every tyrant and of the spirit that murdered the heirs to seize the inheritance, 'this is the heir; come, let us kill him,' Matthew 21:38, the truth that God marks such blood and will require it, the cry of the seventy not unheard, the figure of the murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred, the call to fear the God who avenges innocent blood, the warning against the ambition that destroys all bonds to grasp at power, the figure of the murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred); Abimelech butchering his seventy brothers on one stone to clear his path to the throne, the deliberate murder of Gideon's house (the bloodshed of ambition, 1 Timothy 6:9-10) — the slaughter (seventy on one stone); 'notwithstanding yet Jotham the youngest son of Jerubbaal was left; for he hid himself' (Jotham alone escaping by hiding, God preserving one to pronounce the curse, the love of power leading to the foulest crime yet not utterly prevailing, the lust that kills, James 4:1-2; Matthew 21:38) — the figure (the cruelty of the wicked who shed innocent blood to advance themselves, the providence, the murder, Abimelech made king at Shechem to follow, vv.6) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Abimelech's slaughter of the seventy signify?

sides.position: The murderous cruelty of unbridled ambition, that climbs to power over the blood of kindred - the horror of an ambition that stops at nothing, the seventy butchered on one stone for a usurped crown, ambition unrestrained by the fear of God becoming murderous (1 Timothy 6:9-10), the love of power a root of foulest crime (James 4:1-2), a type of the spirit that murders the heirs to seize the inheritance (Matthew 21:38)

schools.summary: The Shechemites making Abimelech king: 'and all the men of Shechem gathered together, and all the house of Millo, and went, and made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem' — the Shechemites crowned Abimelech, the fruit of the bloody plot (the and all the men of Shechem gathered together, and all the house of Millo, the men gathered, the and went, and made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem, Abimelech made king, the figure, the usurper crowned by the people, the throne built on blood, the truth that the Shechemites set up Abimelech as king, the murder bearing its fruit, Judges 8:23, the figure of the kingdom usurped by violence and confirmed by the people's folly, the crowning of Abimelech, the throne of blood, vv.5); the men of Shechem and Millo gathered (the assembly, Judges 8:23) — the gathering (the men); 'made Abimelech king, by the plain of the pillar that was in Shechem' (Abimelech crowned king, the usurpation, the figure, the kingdom usurped by violence, the providence, the crowning, Jotham's parable from mount Gerizim to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jotham crying out to the men of Shechem from mount Gerizim: 'and when they told it to Jotham, he went and stood in the top of mount Gerizim, and lifted up his voice, and cried, and said unto them, Hearken unto me, ye men of Shechem, that God may hearken unto you' — Jotham, the spared son, cried to the Shechemites from the mount (the and when they told it to Jotham, he went and stood in the top of mount Gerizim, and lifted up his voice, and cried, Jotham cried, the and said unto them, Hearken unto me, ye men of Shechem, that God may hearken unto you, his call to hear, the figure, the lone surviving son crying the truth to the guilty people from the mount of blessing, the truth that Jotham boldly rebuked the Shechemites, that they hear or God hear against them, Deuteronomy 11:29, the figure of the prophet's voice that calls the guilty to hear, the appeal to God's hearing, the cry of Jotham, the call to hear, vv.5); Jotham crying from mount Gerizim (the appeal, Deuteronomy 11:29) — the cry (from Gerizim); 'Hearken unto me, ye men of Shechem, that God may hearken unto you' (the call to hear lest God hear against them, the appeal, the figure, the prophet's voice that calls the guilty to hear, the providence, the cry, the parable of the trees to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jotham's parable of the trees going forth to anoint a king, asking the olive first: 'the trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us' — the parable's design — 'the trees went forth on a time to anoint a king over them; and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us': Jotham frames a fable in which the trees seek a king, first asking the olive (the witnesses noting the significance — the most ancient of parables, framed to expose the folly of the Shechemites in making Abimelech king, the trees a figure of the people seeking a ruler, the worthy and useful trees (the olive, fig, and vine) one by one declining the honour, while only the worthless bramble accepts, the fable holding up a mirror to Shechem, the people's restless desire for a king, the better men unwilling to leave their useful callings for empty rule, the basest grasping at the power the best refuse, the lesson — that those most fit to rule are often least eager for it, content in their fruitful service, while the ambitious and worthless press forward, 'seekest thou great things for thyself? seek them not,' Jeremiah 45:5, the truth that true greatness lies in fruitful service rather than in lordship, and that the desire to rule is no mark of worth, 'whosoever will be great among you, let him be your minister,' Matthew 20:26, the figure — the wisdom that exposes the vanity of ambition and the folly of a people who exalt the unworthy, the parable a glass for every generation that prefers the bramble to the olive, the truth that God's true servants seek not their own exaltation but their appointed fruitfulness, the figure of the folly of seeking a ruler and the truth that the worthy decline what the worthless grasp, the call to be content in fruitful service rather than to grasp at power, the warning that a people who crown the bramble reap its fire, the figure of the folly of seeking a ruler, the worthy declining what the worthless grasp); Jotham framing a fable of the trees seeking a king, the oldest of parables, a mirror held up to the folly of Shechem (the parable's design, Jeremiah 45:5) — the fable (the trees seek a king); 'and they said unto the olive tree, Reign thou over us' (the trees asking the olive, the most useful, to reign, the worthy and fruitful sought first, the better men least eager for empty rule, true greatness in fruitful service, Matthew 20:26) — the figure (the wisdom that exposes the vanity of ambition, the providence, the parable, the olive's refusal to follow, vv.9) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Jotham's parable of the trees seeking a king teach?

sides.position: The folly of seeking a ruler and the truth that the worthy decline what the worthless grasp - the oldest of parables exposing the folly of Shechem in crowning Abimelech, the worthy trees declining the honour while only the bramble accepts, those most fit to rule least eager for it and content in fruitful service (Jeremiah 45:5), true greatness in service rather than lordship (Matthew 20:26)

schools.summary: The vine declining to reign, unwilling to leave its wine that cheers God and man: 'and the vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?' — the fruitful service preferred — 'and the vine said unto them, Should I leave my wine, which cheereth God and man, and go to be promoted over the trees?': the vine, like the olive and fig, refuses the rule, unwilling to forsake its wine (the witnesses noting the significance — the third and crowning refusal of the worthy, the vine prizing its God-given fruitfulness, the wine that gladdens God in the drink offering and man in his joy, above the empty and burdensome honour of lordship, the best of the trees content in their useful callings and unwilling to leave them for rule, the wine that cheers God, used in the offerings, and man, in his gladness, the vine's service better than a throne, the worthy declining what the worthless will grasp, the lesson — that the true servant of God prizes his appointed fruitfulness above place and power, content to bless God and man in his calling rather than to be exalted, 'not slothful in business; fervent in spirit; serving the Lord,' Romans 12:11, the truth that to leave a fruitful service for empty honour is no gain but loss, the useful life better than the lofty seat, 'godliness with contentment is great gain,' 1 Timothy 6:6, the figure — the wine that cheers God and man a figure of the gladness God's true servants bring, fulfilled in the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man, 'I am the true vine,' John 15:1, the truth that the worthy seek not promotion but fruitfulness, and that the desire to rule is no mark of grace, the figure of the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule, the call to be content and fruitful in our callings, the warning that to grasp at rule is to leave the better part, the figure of the worthy who prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule); debates.question: What does the vine's refusal to leave its wine teach?

sides.position: The worthy prize their God-given fruitfulness above the empty honour of rule - the vine prizing its wine that gladdens God and man above lordship, the true servant prizing his appointed fruitfulness above place and power (Romans 12:11), godliness with contentment great gain (1 Timothy 6:6), a figure of the true Vine whose fruit gladdens God and man (John 15:1)

schools.summary: The bramble accepting the rule, offering its shadow and threatening fire to devour the cedars: 'and the bramble said unto the trees, If in truth ye anoint me king over you, then come and put your trust in my shadow: and if not, let fire come out of the bramble, and devour the cedars of Lebanon' — the worthless ruler — 'and the bramble said unto the trees, If in truth ye anoint me king over you, then come and put your trust in my shadow: and if not, let fire come out of the bramble, and devour the cedars of Lebanon': the bramble alone accepts, offering a worthless shadow and threatening fiery ruin (the witnesses noting the significance — the fitting emblem of the unworthy ruler, the bramble that has no fruit and no real shade, yet demands absolute trust and threatens destruction to all who refuse, a low and thorny shrub set over the trees, its shadow a mockery and its fire a real danger, the bramble offering what it cannot give, shelter, and threatening what it can, fire, the worthless ruler exalted, demanding submission and menacing his subjects, Abimelech to the life, the lesson — that the unworthy in power give no true protection but only peril, their rule a snare, their wrath a fire, the people who exalt them put their trust in a shadow that is none and kindle a fire that consumes, 'as a roaring lion... so is a wicked ruler over the poor people,' Proverbs 28:15, the truth that the bramble's fire devouring even the cedars warns that the worthless ruler ruins the great as well as the small, the mutual destruction of usurper and people foretold, 'ye shall be devoured with the sword,' Isaiah 1:20, the figure — the tyrant who can shelter none but can burn all, fulfilled in every usurper whose rule ends in mutual ruin, the bramble and the cedars consumed together, the truth that to crown the worthless is to invite the fire, the figure of the worthless ruler, who can give no shelter but only kindle destruction, the call to beware of exalting the unfit, the warning that the bramble's fire devours those who trust its shadow, the figure of the worthless ruler, who can give no shelter but only kindle destruction); the bramble offering its shadow, a worthless and thorny shrub demanding absolute trust yet giving no real shelter (the unworthy ruler, Proverbs 28:15) — the offer (trust in my shadow); 'and if not, let fire come out of the bramble, and devour the cedars of Lebanon' (the bramble threatening fire to devour even the cedars, the worthless ruler menacing destruction to all, the fire that ruins great and small alike, the mutual ruin of usurper and people foretold, the wicked ruler's wrath, Isaiah 1:20) — the figure (the tyrant who can shelter none but can burn all, the providence, the threat, Jotham applying the parable to Shechem to follow, vv.16-20) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the bramble accepting the rule and threatening fire signify?

sides.position: The worthless ruler, who can give no shelter but only kindle destruction - the fruitless thorny bramble demanding absolute trust yet giving no real shade, offering shelter it cannot give and threatening fire it can, the unworthy in power giving only peril (Proverbs 28:15), the fire devouring even the cedars in mutual ruin (Isaiah 1:20), a figure of the tyrant who shelters none but burns all

themes: And the bramble said unto the trees, If in truth ye anoint me king over you, then come and put your trust in my shadow: and if not, let fire come out of the bramble, and devour the cedars of Lebanon
the bramble accepting the rule, offering its shadow and threatening fire (the crux of the worthless ruler, who can give no shelter but only kindle destruction)
the fitting emblem of the unworthy ruler, the fruitless and thorny bramble that demands absolute trust yet gives no real shade, offering what it cannot (shelter) and threatening what it can (fire)
the unworthy in power giving no protection but only peril, the bramble's fire devouring even the cedars, a figure of the tyrant who shelters none but burns all (Proverbs 28:15; Isaiah 1:20)

schools.summary: Jotham's application testing the Shechemites' dealing with Gideon's house: 'now therefore, if ye have done truly and sincerely, in that ye have made Abimelech king, and if ye have dealt well with Jerubbaal and his house, and have done unto him according to the deserving of his hands' — Jotham set the test of their sincerity and their dealing with Gideon's house (the now therefore, if ye have done truly and sincerely, in that ye have made Abimelech king, the test of sincerity, the and if ye have dealt well with Jerubbaal and his house, and have done unto him according to the deserving of his hands, the test of their dealing, the figure, the appeal to conscience, the deed weighed against Gideon's deserving, the truth that Jotham challenged them to weigh their treachery against Gideon's service, Judges 8:35, the figure of the conscience summoned to judge the deed by its right, the ingratitude exposed, the test of their dealing, the challenge to conscience, vv.5); Jotham testing their sincerity (the challenge, Judges 8:35) — the test (truly and sincerely); 'if ye have dealt well with Jerubbaal and his house' (the test of their dealing with Gideon's house, the conscience summoned, the figure, the conscience summoned to judge the deed, the providence, the challenge, the rehearsal of Gideon's service to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jotham rehearsing Gideon's service in delivering them from Midian: '(for my father fought for you, and adventured his life far, and delivered you out of the hand of Midian' — Jotham recalled Gideon's perilous service for them (the (for my father fought for you, and adventured his life far, Gideon's peril, the and delivered you out of the hand of Midian, the deliverance, the figure, the deliverer's costly service set against the people's treachery, the truth that Gideon risked his life to deliver them, and they repaid his house with blood, Judges 7:1, the figure of the benefactor's service met with ingratitude, the deliverance forgotten, the rehearsal of Gideon's service, the deliverance recalled, vv.16); Gideon adventuring his life for them (the service, Judges 7:1) — the peril (adventured his life); 'delivered you out of the hand of Midian' (Gideon's deliverance recalled, the benefit, the figure, the benefactor's service met with ingratitude, the providence, the rehearsal, the charge of their treachery to follow, vv.18) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jotham charging the Shechemites with the murder of Gideon's sons and the crowning of Abimelech: 'and ye are risen up against my father's house this day, and have slain his sons, threescore and ten persons, upon one stone, and have made Abimelech, the son of his maidservant, king over the men of Shechem, because he is your brother;)' — Jotham charged them with the murder and the base crowning (the and ye are risen up against my father's house this day, and have slain his sons, threescore and ten persons, upon one stone, the murder charged, the and have made Abimelech, the son of his maidservant, king over the men of Shechem, because he is your brother;), the base king crowned, the figure, the treachery laid bare, the murder and the unworthy king, the truth that they slew the seventy and crowned the son of a maidservant for kinship, the basest exchange, Judges 8:31, the figure of the ingratitude that repays deliverance with blood and exalts the unworthy, the charge of treachery, the murder and the crowning, vv.5); the murder of the seventy charged (the treachery, Judges 8:31) — the murder (the seventy slain); 'made Abimelech, the son of his maidservant, king... because he is your brother' (the base king crowned for kinship, the unworthy exalted, the figure, the ingratitude that exalts the unworthy, the providence, the charge, the curse pronounced to follow, vv.19-20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Jotham's ironic conditional blessing on the Shechemites and Abimelech: 'if ye then have dealt truly and sincerely with Jerubbaal and with his house this day, then rejoice ye in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you' — Jotham ironically bade them rejoice in Abimelech if their dealing had been just (the if ye then have dealt truly and sincerely with Jerubbaal and with his house this day, the condition, the then rejoice ye in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you, the ironic blessing, the figure, the irony that grants a blessing the deed makes impossible, the false dealing exposed, the truth that Jotham, knowing their treachery, ironically wished them joy in Abimelech, Judges 9:16, the figure of the irony that lets the guilty judge themselves, the mutual joy that would prove mutual ruin, the ironic blessing, the false dealing exposed, vv.16); the condition of true dealing (the irony, Judges 9:16) — the condition (dealt truly); 'rejoice ye in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you' (the mutual joy ironically wished, the irony, the figure, the irony that lets the guilty judge themselves, the providence, the irony, the curse if they had not dealt truly to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If ye then have dealt truly and sincerely with Jerubbaal and with his house this day, then rejoice ye in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you:

schools.summary: Jotham's curse, that fire should come out from Abimelech and Shechem to devour one another: 'but if not, let fire come out from Abimelech, and devour the men of Shechem, and the house of Millo; and let fire come out from the men of Shechem, and from the house of Millo, and devour Abimelech' — the prophetic curse of mutual ruin — 'but if not, let fire come out from Abimelech, and devour the men of Shechem, and the house of Millo; and let fire come out from the men of Shechem, and from the house of Millo, and devour Abimelech': Jotham invokes a curse of mutual destruction on Abimelech and Shechem (the witnesses noting the significance — the righteous curse, more prophecy than imprecation, foretelling that the wicked who joined in the murder would turn upon and destroy one another, the fire of the bramble parable fulfilled, Abimelech and the Shechemites consuming each other in just judgment, the curse exactly fulfilled within three years, the usurper and the people who crowned him destroying one another, the partners in crime becoming each other's executioners, the lesson — that God turns the wicked against one another, the union of evildoers no lasting bond but a powder-train to mutual ruin, 'the LORD shall... let them fall by their own counsels,' Psalm 5:10, the truth that those who conspire together in sin are bound to fall out and destroy one another, God using their own malice as the instrument of his justice, 'the wicked are overthrown, and are not,' Proverbs 12:7, the figure — the self-consuming nature of the kingdom of evil, divided against itself and falling, the fire that the wicked kindle returning on their own heads, 'his mischief shall return upon his own head,' Psalm 7:16, the truth that the just curse of God on confederate wickedness is sure, the deed itself bearing the seed of its punishment, the figure of the righteous curse fulfilled, the mutual destruction of the wicked who join in evil, the call to fear the God who avenges and turns the wicked against themselves, the warning that partners in sin become partners in ruin, the figure of the righteous curse fulfilled, the mutual destruction of the wicked who join in evil); Jotham invoking fire from Abimelech to devour Shechem, the curse foretelling the usurper's destruction of those who crowned him (the prophetic curse, Psalm 5:10) — the curse (fire devour Shechem); 'and let fire come out from the men of Shechem, and from the house of Millo, and devour Abimelech' (the fire returning from Shechem to devour Abimelech, the partners in crime destroying one another, God turning the wicked against themselves, the mischief returning on their own heads, the wicked overthrown by their own counsels, Proverbs 12:7; Psalm 7:16) — the figure (the self-consuming kingdom of evil divided against itself, the providence, the curse, Jotham fleeing to Beer and Abimelech reigning three years to follow, vv.21-22) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Jotham's curse of mutual fire signify?

sides.position: The righteous curse fulfilled, the mutual destruction of the wicked who join in evil - a prophecy more than imprecation, that the wicked who joined in the murder would turn upon and destroy one another, God turning the wicked against one another so they fall by their own counsels (Psalm 5:10), the confederates in sin destroying each other (Proverbs 12:7), the mischief returning on their own heads (Psalm 7:16)

themes: But if not, let fire come out from Abimelech, and devour the men of Shechem, and the house of Millo; and let fire come out from the men of Shechem, and from the house of Millo, and devour Abimelech

the righteous curse, more prophecy than imprecation, foretelling that the wicked who joined in the murder would turn upon and destroy one another, the fire of the bramble parable fulfilled

God turning the wicked against one another so that they fall by their own counsels, the confederates in sin destroying each other, the mischief returning on their own heads (Psalm 5:10; Proverbs 12:7; Psalm 7:16)

schools.summary: Jotham fleeing to Beer for fear of Abimelech: 'and Jotham ran away, and fled, and went to Beer, and dwelt there, for fear of Abimelech his brother' — Jotham, having spoken, fled for his life (the and Jotham ran away, and fled, and went to Beer, and dwelt there, Jotham fled, the for fear of Abimelech his brother, for fear of Abimelech, the figure, the prophet's word spoken and the prophet withdrawn, the truth left to work, the truth that Jotham, having delivered his message, fled and left the curse to be fulfilled, Judges 9:5, the figure of the faithful witness who speaks and withdraws, the word committed to God, the flight of Jotham, the witness withdrawn, vv.7); Jotham fleeing to Beer (the flight, Judges 9:5) — the flight (to Beer); 'for fear of Abimelech his brother' (Jotham fleeing the murderous Abimelech, the danger, the figure, the faithful witness who speaks and withdraws, the providence, the flight, Abimelech reigning three years to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Abimelech reigning three years over Israel: 'when Abimelech had reigned three years over Israel' — Abimelech's usurped rule lasted but three years (the when Abimelech had reigned three years over Israel, the three years, the figure, the brief reign of the usurper, the judgment delayed but sure, the truth that Abimelech's rule lasted only three years before the curse began to work, Judges 9:20, the figure of the short-lived prosperity of the wicked, the judgment ripening, the reign of Abimelech, the three years, vv.20); Abimelech reigning three years (the brief rule, Judges 9:20) — the reign (three years); the brevity of the reign (the short-lived prosperity, the figure, the short-lived prosperity of the wicked, the providence, the reign, God sending the evil spirit to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: When Abimelech had reigned three years over Israel,

schools.summary: That God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem: 'then God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem; and the men of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech' — the judicial sending — 'then God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the men of Shechem; and the men of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech': God, in righteous judgment, sent a spirit of enmity between the usurper and his confederates (the witnesses noting the significance — the sovereign hand of God in judgment, who sent an 'evil spirit,' a spirit of discord and ill-will, whether an angel of his appointing, or his providential withdrawing of restraint that let their own malice break out, between Abimelech and Shechem, that the partners in crime might turn upon one another and the curse of Jotham be fulfilled, God not the author of their sin, but the righteous Governor who gives the wicked up to their own evil and overrules it for his just ends, the evil spirit the instrument of his judgment, the lesson — that God in his sovereign justice overrules even the sins and enmities of men, turning their malice into the means of their punishment, 'the LORD hath made all things for himself: yea, even the wicked for the day of evil,' Proverbs 16:4, the truth that God may judicially give men over to a spirit of delusion and discord, 'God shall send them strong delusion,' 2 Thessalonians 2:11, or 'give them up' to their own lusts, 'God gave them up,' Romans 1:24, his justice working through their freely-chosen evil, the figure — the righteous Judge who needs no other rod for the wicked than their own wickedness, turning confederates into enemies, the truth that the evil men do is overruled by God to fulfil his word and execute his justice, his holiness untouched and their guilt their own, the figure of the sovereign justice of God who overrules even the evil of men to accomplish his righteous judgment, the call to fear the God who governs even the malice of the wicked, the warning that God can turn a man's own confederates into his destroyers, the figure of the sovereign justice of God, who overrules even the enmity and evil of men to accomplish his righteous judgment); God sending a spirit of discord and ill-will between Abimelech and Shechem, whether by an angel of his appointing or by withdrawing restraint, the instrument of his judgment (the judicial sending, Proverbs 16:4) — the sending (the evil spirit); 'and the men of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech' (the Shechemites turning treacherously against Abimelech, the partners in crime falling out, God giving the wicked up to their own evil and overruling it for his just ends, the evil men do made the means of their punishment, God giving them up, 2 Thessalonians 2:11; Romans 1:24) — the figure (the righteous Judge who needs no other rod for the wicked than their own wickedness, the providence, the treachery, the men of Shechem laying wait and Gaal arising to follow, vv.24-29) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: How is God's sending an evil spirit between Abimelech and Shechem to be understood?

sides.position: The sovereign justice of God who overrules even the evil of men to accomplish his righteous judgment - God not the author of their sin but the righteous Governor who gives the wicked up to their own malice and overrules it, sending a spirit of discord that the confederates turn on one another and Jotham's curse be fulfilled (Proverbs 16:4), giving men judicially over to delusion (2 Thessalonians 2:11) or up to their own lusts (Romans 1:24), his holiness untouched and their guilt their own

God overruling even the sins and enmities of men and turning their malice into the means of their punishment, his holiness untouched and their guilt their own (Proverbs 16:4; 2 Thessalonians 2:11; Romans 1:24)

schools.summary: The blood of the seventy returning upon Abimelech and Shechem: 'that the cruelty done to the threescore and ten sons of Jerubbaal might come, and their blood be laid upon Abimelech their brother, which slew them; and upon the men of Shechem, which aided him in the killing of his brethren' — the blood of the murdered seventy was to be requited on the murderers (the that the cruelty done to the threescore and ten sons of Jerubbaal might come, the cruelty requited, the and their blood be laid upon Abimelech their brother, which slew them; and upon the men of Shechem, which aided him in the killing of his brethren, the blood laid on the guilty, the figure, the innocent blood requited upon the murderer and his helpers, the divine retribution, the truth that God required the blood of the seventy from Abimelech and Shechem alike, Genesis 9:6, the figure of the blood that cries to God and is requited on the shedders, the murderer and his accomplices, the requital of blood, the divine retribution, vv.5); the blood of the seventy laid on Abimelech (the retribution, Genesis 9:6) — the requital (blood on Abimelech); 'upon the men of Shechem, which aided him in the killing of his brethren' (the blood requited on the helpers too, the accomplices, the figure, the blood requited on the shedders and their accomplices, the providence, the requital, the men of Shechem laying wait to follow, vv.25) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: That the cruelty done to the threescore and ten sons of Jerubbaal might come, and their blood be laid upon Abimelech their brother, which slew them; and upon the men of Shechem, which aided him in the killing of his brethren.

schools.summary: The Shechemites setting ambushes and robbing, the breach begun: 'and the men of Shechem set liers in wait for him in the top of the mountains, and they robbed all that came along that way by them: and it was told Abimelech' — the Shechemites began their treachery with ambushes and robbery (the and the men of Shechem set liers in wait for him in the top of the mountains, and they robbed all that came along that way by them, the ambushes and robbery, the and it was told Abimelech, Abimelech informed, the figure, the breach begun, the confederates turning to lawless violence, the truth that the Shechemites began their revolt with ambush and robbery, the curse working, Judges 9:23, the figure of the lawlessness that follows the breach of the wicked, the kingdom of evil dissolving into violence, the ambushes of Shechem, the breach begun, vv.23); the Shechemites setting ambushes and robbing (the lawlessness, Judges 9:23) — the ambushes (in the mountains); 'it was told Abimelech' (Abimelech informed of the revolt, the breach, the figure, the lawlessness that follows the breach of the wicked, the providence, the treachery, Gaal coming to Shechem to follow, vv.26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Gaal coming to Shechem and gaining the people's confidence: 'and Gaal the son of Ebed came with his brethren, and went over to Shechem: and the men of Shechem put their confidence in him' — Gaal arrived and won the trust of the disaffected Shechemites (the and Gaal the son of Ebed came with his brethren, and went over to Shechem, Gaal came, the and the men of Shechem put their confidence in him, their confidence in him, the figure, the new leader of the revolt arising, the people turning to a fresh adventurer, the truth that the Shechemites, having broken with Abimelech, put their trust in Gaal, Judges 9:23, the figure of the people who, having forsaken one bad leader, turn to another, the fickle trust, the rise of Gaal, the confidence misplaced, vv.23); Gaal coming to Shechem (the rise, Judges 9:23) — the coming (Gaal to Shechem); 'the men of Shechem put their confidence in him' (the Shechemites trusting Gaal, the misplaced trust, the figure, the people who turn from one bad leader to another, the providence, the rise, Gaal's boast at the vintage feast to follow, vv.27) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Gill: Abimelech, advanced near by a night march, could attack at sunrise before the inhabitants were aware; he might do as he found occasion.

schools.summary: Gill: Zebul came early to the gate to see how things went; he said, thou seest the shadow of the mountains as if men.

schools.summary: Gill: Gaal looked again toward the mountains for further satisfaction.
Clarke: the plain of Meonenim, 'the plain of the soothsayers.'

themes: See, there come people down by the middle of the land, and another company come along by the plain of Meonenim
the realities of judgment not long hidden by flattering words
the prudent foreseeth the evil... but the simple pass on, and are punished (Prov. 22:3)

schools.summary: Gill: Zebul could put him off no longer, and took the opportunity to upbraid him.
Keil & Delitzsch: the taunt, Where is now thy mouth?

themes: Where is now thy mouth, wherewith thou saidst, Who is Abimelech, that we should serve him?... go out... and fight
the proud shamed when their boastful vaunts are put to the test
pride goeth before destruction, and an haughty spirit before a fall (Prov. 16:18)

schools.summary: Gill: he went at the head of them, gathering as many and in as good order as the time would admit.
Keil & Delitzsch: him fighting with Abimelech.

themes: And Gaal went out before the men of Shechem, and fought with Abimelech
pride hurrying the boaster into a ruinous venture
he that is hasty of spirit exalteth folly (Prov. 14:29)

schools.summary: Gill: Abimelech got the better of him and pursued him closely as he fled; many were overthrown and wounded.

themes: Abimelech chased him... and many were overthrown and wounded, even unto the entering of the gate
the vaunting of the wicked coming to nothing, the proud brought low
they are brought down and fallen: but we are risen, and stand upright (Ps. 20:8)

schools.summary: Gill: Abimelech returned to Arumah, content with the advantage gained; Zebul thrust out Gaal and his brethren.

themes: Abimelech dwelt at Arumah: and Zebul thrust out Gaal and his brethren, that they should not dwell in Shechem
the faithlessness among the wicked who use and discard one another
the expectation of the wicked shall perish (Prov. 10:28)

schools.summary: Gill: this was the day after the battle.
Keil & Delitzsch: the people going out into the field, and Abimelech told.

themes: on the morrow, that the people went out into the field; and they told Abimelech
the false security of the careless caught unawares
while they be folden together as thorns... they shall be devoured (Nah. 1:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the forces he had with him at Arumah.
Keil & Delitzsch: him dividing them into three companies and laying wait.

themes: he... divided them into three companies, and laid wait in the field... and he rose up against them, and smote them
the working-out of Jotham's curse, God using the wicked to punish the wicked
let fire come out from Abimelech, and devour the men of Shechem (Judg. 9:20)

schools.summary: Gill: the company he had particular command of, with great men among them; the two other companies slew the people in the fields.

themes: Abimelech... stood in the entering of the gate of the city: and the two other companies ran upon all the people... and slew them
the ruthlessness of the bloody usurper made an instrument of judgment
the sword without, and terror within, shall destroy (Deut. 32:25)

schools.summary: Gill: he fought against the city all day, throwing stones or arrows into it.
Keil & Delitzsch: he beat down the city and sowed it with salt.

themes: he took the city, and slew the people... and beat down the city, and sowed it with salt
God's judgment thorough and lasting, the place of sin made a barren waste
the whole land thereof is brimstone, and salt... it is not sown, nor beareth (Deut. 29:23)

schools.summary: Gill: the tower was not within the city; the men of it heard the city was taken and sought refuge in the hold of the house of Berith.

themes: all the men of the tower of Shechem... entered into an hold of the house of the god Berith
the folly of trusting a false god for refuge in the day of trouble
their rock is not as our Rock, even our enemies themselves being judges (Deut. 32:31)

schools.summary: Gill: Abimelech had his spies about to observe the men of the tower.
Keil & Delitzsch: it told him they were gathered together.

themes: And it was told Abimelech, that all the men of the tower of Shechem were gathered together
the guilty drawn together to their common ruin
gather... the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them (Matt. 13:30)

schools.summary: Gill: Mount Zalmon, near Shechem, named from the shade of its trees.
Keil & Delitzsch: him cutting a bough and bidding them follow.

themes: cut down a bough from the trees, and... laid it on his shoulder, and said... What ye have seen me do... do as I have done
the power of a leader's example to move men for good or ill
evil men and seducers shall wax worse and worse, deceiving (2 Tim. 3:13)

schools.summary: Gill: the people with their boughs on their shoulders seemed as trees walking; about a thousand men and women died in the fire.

themes: set the hold on fire... so that all the men of the tower of Shechem died also, about a thousand men and women
the fire of Jotham's curse devouring the bloodguilty of Shechem
the LORD thy God is a consuming fire (Deut. 4:24)

schools.summary: Gill: Thebez, near Shechem, which had rebelled against him; he encamped against it and took it.

themes: Then went Abimelech to Thebez, and encamped against Thebez, and took it
the unwearied violence of the tyrant, sin never satisfied
by swearing, and lying, and killing... they break out, and blood toucheth blood (Hos. 4:2)

schools.summary: Gill: the tower of Shechem was without the city, but this within, as towers generally are.
Keil & Delitzsch: all fleeing thither and shutting themselves in.

themes: there was a strong tower within the city, and thither fled all the men and women... and gat them up to the top
the only sure refuge the LORD himself, the true strong tower
the name of the LORD is a strong tower: the righteous runneth into it, and is safe (Prov. 18:10)

schools.summary: Gill: he came with his army to besiege it.
Ellicott: Abimelech was not lacking in physical courage, with his father's impetuosity.

themes: And Abimelech came unto the tower... and went hard unto the door of the tower to burn it with fire
the daring of the wicked hurrying them to their doom
the wicked is snared by the transgression of his lips (Prov. 12:13)

schools.summary: Gill: a piece of the upper millstone, carried up to the top of the tower with other stones to do execution; it all to brake his skull.

themes: And a certain woman cast a piece of a millstone upon Abimelech's head, and all to brake his skull
the proud tyrant felled by the weakest of means, God abasing the mighty
he poureth contempt upon princes, and weakeneth the strength of the mighty (Job 12:21)

schools.summary: Gill: he perceived it a mortal blow, and was in haste lest it be said a woman slew him; the young man thrust him through, and he died.

themes: Draw thy sword, and slay me, that men say not of me, A woman slew him. And his young man thrust him through, and he died
the vanity of the tyrant clinging to pride even in death, yet shamed
before destruction the heart of man is haughty, and before honour is humility (Prov. 18:12)

schools.summary: Gill: those with him, the men of his army, who were Israelites.
Ellicott: the death of a leader was generally enough to break up an army.

themes: when the men of Israel saw that Abimelech was dead, they departed every man unto his place
the works of the wicked standing only while they live
his breath goeth forth... in that very day his thoughts perish (Ps. 146:4)

schools.summary: Gill: it was to the disgrace of his father's character and the hurt of his family.
Keil & Delitzsch: God rendering the wickedness of Abimelech.

themes: Thus God rendered the wickedness of Abimelech, which he did unto his father, in slaying his seventy brethren
the certain justice of God's moral government, the blood returning on his head
whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap (Gal. 6:7); vengeance is mine; I will repay (Rom. 12:19)

schools.summary: Gill: their evil in aiding Abimelech to slay his brethren and making him king after so foul a fact; upon them came the curse of Jotham.

themes: all the evil of the men of Shechem did God render upon their heads: and upon them came the curse of Jotham
God recompensing the partners in sin, his righteous judgment fulfilled
Be not deceived; God is not mocked: for whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he reap (Gal. 6:7)
